passages.explanation: Saul is still breathing threats and murder against the Lord's disciples. He goes to the high priest and gets letters authorising him to arrest any followers of 'the Way' in Damascus — both men and women — and bring them back to Jerusalem. The term 'the Way' is one of the earliest names for the Christian movement. Saul isn't just passively opposed; he's actively hunting believers across borders.

As Saul approaches Damascus, a light from heaven suddenly flashes around him. He falls to the ground and hears a voice: 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?' He asks who's speaking. The answer: 'I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.' This is the critical moment — persecuting the church is persecuting Jesus himself. Jesus tells him to get up and go into the city, where he'll be told what to do. The men with Saul hear the sound but see no one. Saul gets up, opens his eyes, and can't see. They lead him by the hand to Damascus, where he spends three days blind, not eating or drinking.

The Lord speaks to a disciple in Damascus named Ananias in a vision, telling him to go to Straight Street and ask for Saul of Tarsus, who is praying. Saul has had a vision of a man named Ananias coming to restore his sight. Ananias is understandably terrified — he's heard what Saul has done to believers in Jerusalem and knows he's come to Damascus with authority to arrest them. The Lord tells Ananias to go anyway: Saul is a chosen instrument to carry God's name before Gentiles, kings, and the people of Israel. He will be shown how much he must suffer for that name.

Ananias obeys despite his fear. He enters the house, places his hands on Saul, and calls him 'Brother Saul.' The Lord Jesus, who appeared to you on the road, has sent me so you may see again and be filled with the Holy Spirit. Something like scales falls from Saul's eyes. He sees, gets up, is baptised, eats, and regains his strength. The church's greatest persecutor has become a believer.

Saul immediately begins preaching in the Damascus synagogues that Jesus is the Son of God. Everyone who hears him is astonished: isn't this the man who was destroying believers in Jerusalem? Didn't he come here specifically to arrest them? But Saul grows stronger and stronger in his arguments, proving that Jesus is the Messiah. His rabbinical training now serves the gospel instead of opposing it.

After many days, the Jews in Damascus conspire to kill Saul — the hunter has become the hunted. They watch the city gates day and night to catch him. But the disciples help Saul escape by lowering him in a basket through an opening in the city wall at night. Paul later mentions this humiliating escape in 2 Corinthians 11:33.

Saul arrives in Jerusalem and tries to join the disciples, but they're all afraid of him — they don't believe he's really a disciple. Barnabas (the 'Son of Encouragement' from chapter 4) takes Saul to the apostles and vouches for him, explaining what happened on the Damascus road and how Saul preached boldly in Damascus. Saul moves freely in Jerusalem, speaking boldly in the Lord's name and debating with the Greek-speaking Jews. When they try to kill him, the believers send him off to Caesarea and then to Tarsus, his hometown.

Luke gives a brief summary: the church throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria enjoys a period of peace. It's strengthened and encouraged by the Holy Spirit, walking in the fear of the Lord, and growing in numbers. With Saul's conversion, the chief persecutor is now on the other side, and the immediate pressure on the church lifts.

Luke shifts focus back to Peter, who's travelling through the region. In Lydda, he finds a man named Aeneas who's been paralysed and bedridden for eight years. Peter tells him: Jesus Christ heals you — get up and make your bed. Aeneas gets up immediately. All the residents of Lydda and the nearby plain of Sharon see him and turn to the Lord.

In nearby Joppa, a disciple named Tabitha (Dorcas in Greek) — a woman full of good works and charity — becomes sick and dies. Hearing that Peter is in Lydda, the disciples send for him urgently. Peter arrives, sends everyone out of the room, kneels and prays, then turns to the body and says 'Tabitha, get up.' She opens her eyes, sees Peter, and sits up. He helps her to her feet and presents her alive to the believers and widows. News of this spreads throughout Joppa, and many believe. Peter stays in Joppa at the house of Simon the tanner — a detail that matters because tanners worked with dead animals and were considered ritually unclean. Peter is already crossing purity boundaries before his vision in the next chapter.
